candidates.direction_name: Trim The Grouped-Row 128x128 Sibling Export Scratch To The PTX-Style Single Stage
ranking_notes: Round 9 removed the main coherence objection to this family by proving the sibling dispatch itself is flat on the accepted surface rather than a real regression.
This is the highest-priority open frontier family after the live-queue rehydration and it now lines up with the latest measured evidence instead of only historical runs.
It ranks above the PTX-local fallbacks because it attacks the most concrete residual cost on the only newly validated off-branch surface.
candidates.hypothesis: Round 9 turned the grouped-row non-PTX 128x128 sibling from a historical escape hatch into a directly measured live branch: switching the default hot-band dispatch onto that sibling measured 24.18073654 ms, which is only 0.00008011 ms slower than the accepted-base run at 24.18065643 ms and therefore effectively PASS_FLAT on the active search anchor. The NCU headline signature also stayed in the same band instead of collapsing: active warps held at 16.62, long-scoreboard stayed at 7.26, and barrier only moved to 5.48. That means the branch validation step is done. The cleanest next move is the historically positive follow-on inside that family: replace the sibling's heavier generic export scratch lifetime with the PTX-style single-stage per-warp scratch path that previously improved the sibling branch from 24.449024 ms to 24.422464 ms. Now that the sibling route is live in the current tree, this bounded export-side trim is the highest-upside next step without reopening a broader family pivot.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Shared export and writeback overhead inside the grouped-row non-PTX 128x128 sibling, especially the sibling branch's heavier export scratch lifetime after the hot-band dispatch has already moved onto that family.
candidates.risk: Medium. The family is bounded and historically positive, but it still leaves the absolute best-known PTX run behind unless the sibling export trim converts the newly validated flat branch into a real win.

candidates.direction_name: Continue The Active PTX One-K 128x128 Control-Path Exploit
ranking_notes: This remains the best active-PTX fallback because the branch still owns the absolute best runtime even after the sibling pivot validated as flat.
It stays below the sibling export trim because recent PTX-local follow-ups already spent two rounds for one tiny win and one slight regression.
Keeping it second preserves a live way back onto the best-known PTX surface if the sibling follow-on fails.
candidates.hypothesis: The active PTX branch still owns the best-known custom runtime at 24.16435242 ms, so it cannot be dropped from the queue just because the last two PTX-local tweaks flattened out. The right interpretation of rounds 7 through 9 is narrower: the export-address cleanup harvested a tiny writeback win, the follow-up issue-grouping retime flattened, and the sibling dispatch pivot then validated an off-branch alternative without beating the PTX best. That leaves one coherent PTX fallback alive: a bounded control-path exploit that stays on the best-known PTX surface but avoids simply replaying the same export cleanup or handoff micro-change. If the sibling export trim fails to convert its flat branch into a win, this remains the strongest on-surface family to revisit.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Residual PTX hot-band control-path overhead on the best-known 128x128 PTX surface, especially helper lifetime and consume-order friction that the export-address cleanup did not remove.
candidates.risk: Moderate. The family has one historical PASS_WIN and multiple later PASS_FLAT outcomes, so another attempt must be materially different from the already-baked control-order tweaks or it will likely collapse back into noise.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.164352 ms best-known PTX run
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if another bounded PTX control-path tweak again behaves like PASS_FLAT noise without improving the 24.164352 ms best-known PTX band.

ranking_notes: This remains a coherent hold-in-queue family because the scheduler-facing counters are still flat enough that a bounded handoff retime could matter if the higher-ranked families fail.
It ranks below the broader PTX control family because the likely upside is smaller and the current tree already contains the strongest obvious handoff cleanup.
Keeping it third preserves a materially different scheduler lever without reopening the already rejected geometry pivots.
candidates.hypothesis: The barrier and handoff family remains worth carrying only as a narrower scheduler fallback, not as the next recommendation. The evidence is mixed but still coherent enough to keep alive: the PTX export cleanup improved barrier slightly on the best-known run, round 8 then flattened without changing the stall mix much, and round 9's sibling branch did not reveal a radically better occupancy or scoreboard signature. That means a carefully bounded wait-group and handoff retime is still a plausible third option if both the sibling export trim and the broader PTX control family fail to convert. It stays third because the current source already includes the strongest obvious one-sync cleanup, so another retime has less headroom than the two families above.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Residual wait-group and barrier cadence in the hot-band steady-state loop, either on the active PTX branch or as a scheduler lens for comparing the sibling follow-on.
candidates.risk: Moderate. A handoff retime is bounded, but the source already absorbed the strongest obvious cleanup and another retime can easily trade barrier for scoreboard without moving runtime.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.164352 ms best-known PTX run
